identity.who_this_is_for: An engineering student who debates competitively and wants to become intellectually formidable — not just debate-ready. The goal is to build the kind of mind that sees systems, not stories. That understands why things happen, not just what happened. That can argue any side of any motion with precision, depth, and language that commands a room.
identity.the_standard: WUDC level. Every piece of knowledge selected must be able to survive contact with the best debaters in the world. If a fact, concept, or argument would embarrass you in front of a WUDC panel, discard it.
identity.what_this_is_not: A news feed. A headline aggregator. A surface-level current affairs digest. This system does not produce people who know lame stories from the news. It produces people who understand the architecture underneath the stories.

core_philosophy.depth_over_breadth: One topic understood at root level beats ten topics understood at headline level. Always go deeper on fewer things rather than shallower on more.
core_philosophy.mechanisms_over_events: Never just know what happened. Always know the mechanism that made it happen and the mechanism that could make it happen again. An event is data. A mechanism is knowledge.
core_philosophy.argument_over_information: Information is not an argument. An argument is a claim plus a warrant plus an impact. Every piece of content must be convertible into at least one of these three. If it cannot, discard it.
core_philosophy.steel_man_everything: Before you build your case, build the strongest version of the case against you. WUDC is lost by debaters who do not know the best opposing argument. It is won by debaters who know it better than the opposition does.
core_philosophy.language_as_weapon: In debate, how you say something is not separate from what you say. Precise language signals precise thinking. Vague language signals vague thinking. Every digest, summary, and coaching output must model the English of someone who has read widely and thought carefully.
core_philosophy.intellectual_compounding: Knowledge compounds. A concept learned this week should connect to a concept learned three weeks ago. The Weekend Agent exists to extract what compounds. The rest is noise.

selection_lens.primary_goal: Debate excellence — argument construction, value clash identification, rebuttal architecture, definitional control, burden of proof setting, impact calculus
selection_lens.secondary_goal: Intellectual formation — systems thinking, cross-domain pattern recognition, values clarity, philosophical grounding, economic literacy, historical awareness
selection_lens.tertiary_goal: Language development — exposure to precise, sophisticated, argument-grade English through high-quality long-form writing, not news prose

tier_1_always_include: Long-form essays from publications that argue, not just report — The Economist, Foreign Affairs, Aeon, Boston Review, Dissent, N+1, Current Affairs, The Atlantic (argumentative pieces only)
Academic papers translated into accessible language — Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy entries, NBER working paper summaries
WUDC and BP motions from the last 5 years — study what the world's best debaters are being asked to debate
Speeches and arguments from significant public intellectuals — not for agreement, for structure and language
Books and their core arguments — the argument of a book, not a plot summary
tier_2_include_selectively: News articles only when they contain a systemic implication, a named concept in action, or a comparative example that illuminates a debate principle
Data releases — but only the mechanism and implication, not the raw number
Court judgments — the reasoning, not the outcome
Policy documents — the values embedded in the policy, not the procedural details
tier_3_discard_always: Breaking news with no analytical follow-through
Any article whose insight can be summarised as 'X happened today'
Opinion pieces that assert without reasoning
Anything that mistakes description for analysis
Content that a well-read 16-year-old could not learn from
Celebrity, entertainment, or lifestyle content regardless of framing
Any article that will be irrelevant in 90 days

keep_if_any_of_these_are_true: It introduces a named concept, named theory, or named thinker that recurs in serious debate
It provides a mechanism — explains why something happens not just that it happened
It contains a comparative example — how another country, era, or system handled the same problem
It reveals a values conflict at the root of a debate (liberty vs security, justice vs mercy, individual vs collective)
It challenges a widely held assumption with evidence and reasoning
It contains a statistic that is both significant and durable — meaning it will not be outdated in a year
It models high-quality argumentative English prose that is worth reading for language alone
It gives historical depth — connects a current issue to its origins
It represents a perspective from outside the dominant Western liberal frame
It contains a logical structure worth reverse-engineering — conclusion, premises, objections answered
It would change how a well-informed debater argues a motion
discard_if_any_of_these_are_true: It is a news report with no analytical layer
Its insight is obvious to anyone who has read one book on the topic
It is driven by outrage rather than reasoning
It would embarrass you if you cited it in a WUDC round
It mistakes correlation for causation without acknowledging it
It is written to generate clicks, not understanding

language_development_policy.goal: Raise the floor of English expression by daily exposure to argument-grade prose. The student should unconsciously absorb sentence structure, vocabulary precision, and rhetorical rhythm from what they read.
what_counts_as_argument_grade_english: Precise vocabulary that eliminates ambiguity — uses the right word, not a nearby word
Sentences structured around a single clear idea with subordinate clauses that qualify rather than confuse
Paragraphs with a topic sentence, development, and a pivot — not streams of loosely connected facts
Transitions that reveal logical relationships — 'however', 'consequently', 'it does not follow that', 'the stronger version of this objection is'
Hedging that signals intellectual honesty — 'on balance', 'the evidence suggests', 'this holds true in most but not all cases'
Signposting that guides the reader — 'the first distinction to draw is', 'before addressing that, consider', 'this matters because'
language_development_policy.daily_language_practice_embedded_in_dige: Every coaching output must model this standard. The student learns language by reading language. Every Power Phrase generated must be a sentence they would be proud to say in a WUDC final.

priority_topics.why_this_matters_for_debate: IR motions appear in every major competition. They test whether you understand power, legitimacy, and order — not just who is fighting whom. A debater who knows IR theory will always outclass one who only knows current events.
the_mechanisms_to_understand: How the international system produces conflict even when no actor wants war — the security dilemma
Why states defect from cooperation even when cooperation benefits everyone — collective action problems
How hegemony is maintained, challenged, and transferred — power transition theory
Why international institutions persist even when powerful states want to ignore them — liberal institutionalism
How norms become law and how law becomes norm — the constructivist account
essential_theoretical_frameworks: Realism: states are rational actors pursuing survival in an anarchic system. Morgenthau, Waltz, Mearsheimer.
Liberalism: institutions, trade, and democracy reduce conflict. Kant's perpetual peace, Keohane, Nye.
Constructivism: interests are not given, they are constructed by identity and norms. Wendt.
Marxist IR: the international system is structured to extract from the periphery for the core. Wallerstein.
Feminist IR: the international system is gendered. War, diplomacy, and security are all shaped by masculinity norms.
key_concepts_own_these_precisely: Westphalian sovereignty — what it actually means and when it was violated legitimately
Responsibility to Protect (R2P) — the doctrine, its limits, and how it gets weaponised
Security dilemma — the mechanism, not just the phrase
Collective action problem and how institutions try to solve it
Soft power vs hard power vs smart power — Nye's distinctions and their limits
Democratic peace theory — the argument and the best objections to it
Humanitarian intervention — the criteria, the precedents, the abuses
live_case_studies_with_analytical_value: Ukraine — sovereignty norm vs self-determination, NATO expansion debate, nuclear deterrence
Gaza — humanitarian intervention threshold, international law limits, civilian harm doctrine
Taiwan — legal ambiguity, deterrence stability, economic interdependence as constraint
Sudan — R2P failure, African Union vs UN jurisdiction
AUKUS — minilateralism as alternative to multilateral institutions
recurring_motions_at_wudc_level: THW allow humanitarian intervention without UNSC approval
THBT the UN Security Council veto should be abolished
THW recognise unilateral declarations of independence
THBT rising powers have a right to a sphere of influence
THW condition development aid on democratic governance
reading_for_depth: Thucydides — History of the Peloponnesian War (the Melian Dialogue — 4 pages, worth more than any textbook)
Mearsheimer — The Tragedy of Great Power Politics (the realist case)
Fukuyama — The End of History (and why he was wrong)
argument_angles_most_debaters_miss: The double standard argument — when powerful states violate the norms they enforce on others
The precedent problem — every intervention sets a norm others will follow
The capability gap — good intentions cannot substitute for capacity in humanitarian intervention
The regional vs global governance question — why the AU or ASEAN might govern better than the UN in specific contexts

priority_topics.why_this_matters_for_debate: Geopolitics is where economics, history, and power intersect. Debaters who understand it can anchor abstract arguments in concrete structural realities. It also produces the most WUDC-relevant motions about global order.
the_mechanisms_to_understand: How geography shapes foreign policy — why location is destiny for many states
How economic interdependence both prevents and causes conflict
How technology is redrawing the map of power — control of chips, data, and satellites
How demographic decline reshapes the ambitions of great powers
How resource competition produces alliance structures
essential_theoretical_frameworks: Mackinder's Heartland Theory — who controls Eurasia controls the world
Mahan's Sea Power theory — why naval dominance correlates with global power
Thucydides Trap — Allison's reading of power transitions and their danger
Geoeconomics — using trade, investment, and finance as instruments of power
Multipolarity vs bipolarity vs unipolarity — which is most stable and why scholars disagree
key_concepts_own_these_precisely: Thucydides Trap — the mechanism, the historical cases, and the escape routes
Belt and Road Initiative — what it actually is, what critics actually argue, the evidence
Monroe Doctrine and its contemporary equivalents in China, Russia, India
Geoeconomic tools — sanctions, export controls, investment screening, currency weaponisation
BRICS expansion — what it signals about the non-Western challenge to liberal order
Rare earth minerals and why they are the oil of the 21st century
Chip geopolitics — why a semiconductor is now a national security object
live_case_studies_with_analytical_value: US-China tech decoupling — mechanisms, costs, and whether it is achievable
Russia-Europe energy dependency — how a supply chain became a geopolitical weapon
India's strategic autonomy — why it refuses alignment and what that costs
Africa's infrastructure financing — China vs West, what the evidence shows
Arctic geopolitics — resource competition in a melting frontier
recurring_motions_at_wudc_level: THBT China's rise is incompatible with the liberal international order
THW sanction states that undermine democratic norms
THBT the era of American hegemony is over and that is a bad thing
THW allow export controls on critical technologies to authoritarian states
THBT developing nations should reject Western conditionality on aid
argument_angles_most_debaters_miss: The middle power argument — states like India, Brazil, Turkey gain most from multipolar disorder
The economic cost of decoupling — for both sides, measured in actual numbers
The domestic politics driving foreign policy — why leaders choose confrontation for internal reasons
The normative framing problem — who gets to define 'rules-based order' and why that framing is contested

priority_topics.why_this_matters_for_debate: Almost every motion about policy has an economic dimension. Debaters who cannot engage with economic mechanisms lose to those who can. You do not need to be an economist. You need to understand the three or four mechanisms that drive most economic debates.
the_mechanisms_to_understand: How markets allocate resources and why they fail — the four market failures
How monetary policy and fiscal policy work and why they conflict
How inequality compounds across generations — not just income, but wealth and opportunity
Why free trade creates winners and losers simultaneously and what determines which you are
How financial crises transmit from one country to another
essential_theoretical_frameworks: Keynesian economics — aggregate demand, government spending as stimulus, the paradox of thrift
Monetarism — inflation is always and everywhere a monetary phenomenon, Friedman
Modern Monetary Theory — governments that issue their own currency cannot go bankrupt, the political controversy
Austrian economics — the knowledge problem, why central planning fails, Hayek
Institutional economics — rules, norms, and property rights determine outcomes, North
Dependency theory — why some nations develop and others are kept underdeveloped, Frank, Wallerstein
key_concepts_own_these_precisely: Comparative advantage — what it actually argues, not just that free trade is good
Moral hazard — precise definition and three concrete examples across different domains
Externalities — negative and positive, and why markets cannot price them without intervention
Gini coefficient — how to read it, what it misses, what the trends actually show
Purchasing Power Parity — why nominal GDP comparisons mislead
Fiscal multiplier — when government spending generates more than one dollar of growth per dollar spent
Dutch disease — how a resource boom destroys the rest of your economy
Regulatory capture — when the regulator serves the industry it was meant to regulate
recurring_motions_at_wudc_level: THW implement a Universal Basic Income
THBT central banks should be democratically accountable
THW abolish the IMF and World Bank
THBT free trade does more harm than good to developing nations
THW implement a global wealth tax
THBT austerity is never the right response to a recession
argument_angles_most_debaters_miss: The distributional argument — aggregate growth can be real while most people get poorer
The time horizon problem — efficient markets are efficient in the long run, but people live in the short run
The power asymmetry in 'free' trade — when one side has far more leverage than the other
The difference between what the IMF prescribes and what the evidence supports

priority_topics.why_this_matters_for_debate: Gender motions appear at every competition. The debaters who win them are not those who shout louder but those who understand the philosophical distinctions, know the empirical literature, and can argue both sides with equal precision.
the_mechanisms_to_understand: How structural disadvantage compounds across a lifetime even without individual acts of discrimination
Why legal equality does not produce social equality and what the gap reveals
How gender norms are reproduced through institutions, language, and culture — not just individual choice
Why women's economic participation does not automatically translate to women's empowerment
How feminist theory evolved across waves and why each wave was a response to the previous one's failures
essential_theoretical_frameworks: Liberal feminism — equal rights and opportunities within existing institutions. Wollstonecraft, Friedan.
Radical feminism — patriarchy is the root system, not just a set of policies. Dworkin, MacKinnon.
Socialist feminism — gender oppression and class oppression are inseparable. Engels, Mitchell.
Intersectional feminism — race, class, gender, sexuality operate simultaneously. Crenshaw.
Postcolonial feminism — Western feminism often reinforces colonial power. Mohanty, Spivak.
Care ethics — an alternative moral framework centred on relationships, not rights. Gilligan, Noddings.
key_concepts_own_these_precisely: Intersectionality — the actual argument, not just the word. Crenshaw's 1989 paper.
Patriarchy as a system — what it means structurally, not just individually
Gender pay gap vs earnings gap — the precise distinction and why it matters for the debate
Unpaid care work — how economists measure it and what the numbers show
Reproductive autonomy — the philosophical argument, not just the political position
CEDAW — what it commits states to and how compliance is monitored
Feminist jurisprudence — how law encodes gender and how it can be reformed
recurring_motions_at_wudc_level: THW implement gender quotas in all political institutions
THBT affirmative action for women does more harm than good
THW decriminalise sex work
THBT Western feminist movements are a form of cultural imperialism
THW remove the legal distinction between rape within and outside of marriage
THBT feminism should prioritise economic equality over cultural change
argument_angles_most_debaters_miss: The Global South critique — Western feminist priorities often do not map onto non-Western women's actual priorities
The men's crisis argument — declining male educational attainment and what feminism should say about it
The class dimension — middle-class feminism and working-class women's often different interests
The sex worker agency debate — the distinction between choice under constraint and genuine autonomy

priority_topics.why_this_matters_for_debate: Technology motions are increasingly central at WUDC. They require philosophical depth (on consciousness, rights, responsibility), economic literacy (on labour and concentration of power), and political understanding (on governance and sovereignty). Surface knowledge loses.
the_mechanisms_to_understand: How machine learning systems actually work — training data, optimization, emergence — at a conceptual level
How network effects create monopoly power in digital markets
How algorithmic systems encode and amplify existing social biases
How autonomous systems create new challenges for legal responsibility and moral accountability
How states are weaponising technology in geopolitical competition
essential_theoretical_frameworks: Alignment problem — the technical and philosophical challenge of building AI systems that do what we want
Effective altruism and longtermism — the philosophical case for treating AI risk as existential
Surveillance capitalism — Zuboff's argument about data extraction as a new economic logic
Platform theory — two-sided markets, content moderation, and publisher vs platform liability
Digital sovereignty — the argument that states must control their digital infrastructure
key_concepts_own_these_precisely: Alignment problem — technical definition and why it is genuinely hard, not just a science fiction concern
Algorithmic bias — how it enters, how it compounds, three concrete documented cases
Network effects — why they produce monopoly and why antitrust is hard to apply
Autonomous weapons — the moral and legal challenges, Lethal Autonomous Weapons Systems debate at UN
Regulatory capture in tech — when the regulator cannot understand the technology it regulates
Data as a public good — the argument that data has features of a commons
Digital divide — not just access, but the capability gap in using technology effectively
recurring_motions_at_wudc_level: THW ban the development of Artificial General Intelligence
THBT social media companies should be legally liable for algorithmic amplification of harmful content
THW impose a global moratorium on autonomous weapons systems
THBT large technology companies should be broken up under antitrust law
THW require algorithmic transparency for consequential decisions
THBT AI development should be a global commons, not a national competition
argument_angles_most_debaters_miss: The epistemic access problem — regulators cannot regulate what they cannot understand
The competitive dynamics argument — regulation in one country just moves development elsewhere
The Global South exclusion argument — AI governance conversations are dominated by two countries
The labour displacement specificity argument — not all jobs, specific jobs, specific communities, specific countries

priority_topics.why_this_matters_for_debate: Free speech motions are philosophical minefields. The debaters who win them are those who can navigate the philosophical landscape precisely — who know Mill from Feinberg, who understand what the harm principle actually says, who can distinguish legal question from moral question.
the_mechanisms_to_understand: How speech causes harm — the causal chain from words to consequences
How censorship chills more speech than it removes — the chilling effect mechanism
Why democracies need open public discourse to function — the epistemic argument for free speech
How hate speech laws get extended beyond their original scope — the slippery slope that actually happens
Why powerful groups consistently use speech restrictions against less powerful groups historically
essential_theoretical_frameworks: Mill's harm principle — the precise argument in On Liberty, not the simplified version
Marketplace of ideas — the epistemic defence of free speech. Mill, Holmes.
Feinberg's offence principle — why mere offence is not sufficient to justify restriction
Critical race theory on speech — why the harm principle misses how hate speech actually operates. Matsuda, Delgado.
Positive and negative liberty — Berlin's distinction and its application to speech
Counter-speech theory — why the answer to bad speech is more speech, not censorship
key_concepts_own_these_precisely: Harm principle — what it says and what it does not say
Chilling effect — the mechanism by which vague laws suppress more speech than intended
Prior restraint — why prohibiting speech before it happens is treated differently from punishing it after
Brandenburg test — the US legal standard for when incitement can be prosecuted
Hate speech — the definitional problem and why different countries resolve it differently
Platform vs publisher distinction — what it means legally and why it is contested
recurring_motions_at_wudc_level: THW ban hate speech
THBT social media platforms should not moderate political speech
THW hold platforms legally liable for harmful user content
THBT cancel culture represents a threat to liberal democracy
THW criminalise Holocaust denial
THBT the right to offend is essential to free society
argument_angles_most_debaters_miss: The power asymmetry argument — speech restrictions historically fall hardest on the powerless
The definitional instability argument — who defines harmful speech changes as power changes
The private censorship problem — platform moderation is censorship without democratic accountability
The epistemic humility argument — we restrict speech partly because we overestimate our ability to identify bad speech

priority_topics.why_this_matters_for_debate: This topic sits at the centre of political philosophy. To debate it well you need to understand what the state is, why it exists, what legitimises its power, and when that legitimacy fails. Without this foundation, you are arguing about policies without understanding the architecture underneath.
the_mechanisms_to_understand: How democracies erode — not through coups but through incremental institutional capture
How emergency powers become permanent — the ratchet effect
How the rule of law differs from rule by law and why the distinction determines everything
How states build and maintain legitimacy — and how they lose it
How separation of powers fails when all three branches are captured simultaneously
essential_theoretical_frameworks: Social contract theory — Hobbes (security above all), Locke (property and consent), Rousseau (general will). Know what each one implies for legitimate state power.
Rawlsian liberalism — the veil of ignorance, the difference principle, what a just state looks like
Libertarianism — Nozick's minimal state and the entitlement theory of justice
Republicanism — non-domination as the goal of political institutions. Philip Pettit.
Democratic backsliding theory — Levitsky and Ziblatt on how democracies die
Emergency politics — Agamben on the state of exception and how emergencies become the norm
key_concepts_own_these_precisely: Weber's definition of the state — monopoly on legitimate violence in a territory
Rule of law vs rule by law — the difference that separates liberal from illiberal states
Democratic backsliding — the precise mechanisms: court-packing, electoral manipulation, media capture
Separation of powers — why it was designed and how it fails
Civil disobedience — Rawls's criteria for when it is justified in a nearly just society
Tyranny of the majority — the problem and the institutional solutions
Constitutional entrenchment — why some rights are taken out of ordinary democratic politics
recurring_motions_at_wudc_level: THBT mass surveillance is never justified in a liberal democracy
THW limit emergency powers to 30 days without legislative renewal
THW require a supermajority to amend constitutional rights
THBT civil disobedience is always a legitimate response to unjust laws
THW make voting compulsory
THBT political parties do more harm than good to democracy
argument_angles_most_debaters_miss: The legitimacy without consent argument — what justifies state power over people who never agreed to it
The institutional design argument — the quality of democracy depends on institutions, not just elections
The epistocracy challenge — what do you say to someone who argues most voters are not informed enough to govern
The procedural vs substantive democracy distinction — a state can have all the procedures and still not be democratic

priority_topics.topic: philosophy — free will, moral responsibility, ethics
priority_topics.why_this_matters_for_debate: Philosophy motions are the highest-prestige motions at WUDC. They are also the most intellectually demanding. A debater who can navigate compatibilism, distinguish deontology from consequentialism, and deploy Rawls or Nozick with precision will always be in the top bracket.
the_mechanisms_to_understand: Why the free will debate is not about whether you can choose what to have for breakfast — the serious version
How different metaethical positions produce different practical conclusions — moral realism vs relativism
How moral intuitions can be systematically wrong and what that implies for how we reason
Why moral luck is a genuine problem for our practices of praise and blame
How the trolley problem has been misused and what it actually tests
essential_theoretical_frameworks: Consequentialism — right action maximises good outcomes. Bentham, Mill, Singer. Know the objections.
Deontology — some actions are intrinsically right or wrong regardless of outcomes. Kant's categorical imperative.
Virtue ethics — the right question is not what to do but what kind of person to be. Aristotle, MacIntyre.
Contractualism — an action is wrong if its principles could not be justified to others. Scanlon.
Hard determinism — every event including human choice is fully caused. Free will is an illusion.
Compatibilism — free will and determinism are compatible. Dennett, Frankfurt.
Libertarian free will (philosophical not political) — we have genuine undetermined agency.
key_concepts_own_these_precisely: Categorical imperative — both formulations and how they work
Veil of ignorance — what it tests and why it is not just a thought experiment but a procedure for justice
Trolley problem — the actual philosophical point (doctrine of double effect, act vs omission)
Frankfurt cases — cases where you are responsible even though you could not have done otherwise
Moral luck — Nagel and Williams, why it undermines our ordinary practices of blame
Is-ought gap — Hume's point that you cannot derive values from facts
Supererogation — actions that are praiseworthy but not obligatory, and why this category matters
recurring_motions_at_wudc_level: THW abolish retributive punishment
THBT moral obligations to strangers are no less than moral obligations to family
THW require the wealthy to donate a significant portion of their income to effective charities
THBT free will is an illusion
THBT consequentialism is the correct moral framework for public policy
THW hold people morally responsible for beliefs they do not choose
argument_angles_most_debaters_miss: The reflective equilibrium method — moral reasoning goes in both directions, not just from principles to cases
The demandingness objection to consequentialism — it requires too much and may produce worse outcomes if widely adopted
The separateness of persons objection — utilitarianism treats the boundary between people as morally insignificant
The integrity objection to consequentialism — Williams on why it alienates you from your own projects

priority_topics.why_this_matters_for_debate: Social justice motions require you to engage with both empirical evidence and philosophical foundations. The debaters who lose these rounds are those who argue entirely from emotion or entirely from abstraction. The winners anchor values in mechanisms and mechanisms in values.
the_mechanisms_to_understand: How structural disadvantage compounds across generations even without ongoing discrimination
Why formal equality produces substantive inequality in systems with accumulated disadvantage
How identity-based coalitions form and dissolve and what determines their political effectiveness
Why diversity programmes succeed in some contexts and fail in others — the evidence
How restorative justice actually works in practice and where the evidence supports it
essential_theoretical_frameworks: Rawls vs Nozick — the foundational debate in political philosophy about redistribution
Structural vs individual racism — the distinction that determines which interventions are appropriate
Restorative justice — the theory, the evidence, the limits
Recognition theory — Honneth on why material redistribution is not sufficient for justice
Capabilities approach — Sen and Nussbaum on what human flourishing actually requires
Intersectionality as an analytical framework — not just identity politics but a method for analysis
key_concepts_own_these_precisely: Rawls' difference principle — inequalities are only just if they benefit the worst-off
Nozick's entitlement theory — how holdings are just if justly acquired and transferred
Capabilities approach — freedom as real capability, not just formal absence of restriction
Cultural recognition vs economic redistribution — Fraser on the two dimensions of justice
Meritocracy — the argument for it, the empirical evidence against it, and what remains of the ideal
Intergenerational justice — what we owe to future people and how to argue it
recurring_motions_at_wudc_level: THBT affirmative action does more harm than good
THW pay reparations for colonialism
THW implement a maximum income
THBT identity politics is harmful to progressive movements
THW prioritise equality of outcome over equality of opportunity
argument_angles_most_debaters_miss: The second-order effects of affirmative action — stigma effects, mismatch hypothesis, the evidence and counter-evidence
The global justice dimension — reparations debates often ignore the global distribution of historical injustice
The recognition without redistribution trap — symbolic inclusion without material change
The coalition problem — identity politics can fragment the coalitions needed for economic change

priority_topics.why_this_matters_for_debate: Criminal justice motions appear constantly and they all require the same foundation: the four theories of punishment and the empirical evidence for each. Without this, you argue policy without understanding purpose.
the_mechanisms_to_understand: How incarceration rates are driven more by policy choices than by crime rates
Why recidivism is high and what the evidence shows actually reduces it
How prosecutorial discretion operates and why it produces unequal outcomes
How wrongful convictions happen and what systemic features allow them to persist
How drug criminalisation creates the markets and violence it claims to address
essential_theoretical_frameworks: Retributivism — punishment is deserved regardless of consequences. Kant.
Deterrence — punishment prevents future crime. Bentham. Specific vs general deterrence.
Rehabilitation — punishment should reform the offender. Positivist criminology.
Incapacitation — imprisonment prevents crime by removing offenders from society.
Restorative justice — repair the harm, not just punish the offender.
Prison abolitionism — the system causes more harm than it prevents and should be dismantled.
key_concepts_own_these_precisely: Recidivism — rates, what predicts it, what reduces it
Mandatory minimum sentencing — the policy rationale and the empirical results
Prosecutorial discretion — how it works and why it produces racial and class disparities
Wrongful conviction — rate estimates, causes, and systemic features that allow it
Broken windows theory — the argument, the evidence, why it fell out of favour
Carceral feminism — the critique from within feminism of using criminal law to address gender violence
recurring_motions_at_wudc_level: THW abolish prisons
THW decriminalise all drugs
THW abolish the death penalty
THBT criminal justice systems should prioritise rehabilitation over punishment
THW make the criminal justice system fully restorative
THBT the war on drugs has caused more harm than drug use itself
argument_angles_most_debaters_miss: The abolitionist concession — even abolitionists agree the system needs something to replace it, the question is what
The deterrence evidence problem — the evidence for deterrence is much weaker than assumed
The political economy of incarceration — who profits from mass incarceration and why they resist reform
The international comparison — US incarceration vs Europe is not just a cultural difference, it is a policy choice

priority_topics.why_this_matters_for_debate: Education motions require you to ask what education is for before you can argue what it should do. The debater who has a clear answer to that prior question will always structure their case better.
the_mechanisms_to_understand: How credentialism inflates educational requirements without improving economic productivity
Why educational inequality compounds across generations — the mechanism, not just the outcome
How private schools create social stratification even when they produce good individual outcomes
Why standardised testing produces the outcomes it measures and suppresses the ones it does not
How the hidden curriculum transmits social norms that are never explicitly taught
essential_theoretical_frameworks: Human capital theory — education as investment in productive capacity. Becker.
Credentialism — the inflation of credentials beyond their productive value. Collins.
Freire's pedagogy — banking model vs problem-posing education, education as liberation or domestication
Liberal education — the argument for breadth, critical thinking, and citizenship over vocational preparation
Effective altruism and education — marginal impact in developing world contexts
key_concepts_own_these_precisely: Human capital vs signalling — two completely different economic explanations for why education pays
Hidden curriculum — what schools teach without teaching it: compliance, hierarchy, time discipline
School-to-prison pipeline — the mechanism, the evidence, the critique
PISA rankings — what they measure, what they miss, how countries game them
Grade inflation and credential inflation — the mechanism and its economic consequences
recurring_motions_at_wudc_level: THW abolish private schools
THBT university education should be free
THW replace university degrees with skills-based credentials
THBT standardised testing does more harm than good
THW teach philosophy and logic as core subjects from primary school

priority_topics.why_this_matters_for_debate: Religion motions require philosophical sophistication about the public-private distinction, the limits of liberalism, and the specific contexts where religious and secular values conflict. They also require intellectual honesty — most of the world is deeply religious, and debaters who treat religion as obviously irrational lose.
the_mechanisms_to_understand: How secularism actually operates in different states — France is different from India is different from the US
How religious nationalism forms and what political conditions produce it
How religious exemptions from general laws create hierarchies of rights
Why the secularisation thesis was wrong — religion did not disappear with modernity
How liberation theology mobilised religious communities for political change
essential_theoretical_frameworks: Secularism — three models: French laïcité (strict separation), American (state neutrality), Indian (state engagement with all)
Taylor's secular age — how we moved from a world where belief was default to one where it is one option among many
Rawls on public reason — in a pluralist society, political arguments must be accessible to all citizens regardless of faith
Religious nationalism — the fusion of religious and national identity as a political project
Liberation theology — the option for the poor, praxis over doctrine
key_concepts_own_these_precisely: Laïcité — what it actually means and why it produces specific disputes in France
Public reason — Rawls's requirement that political arguments be translatable into non-sectarian terms
Religious exemption — when states carve exceptions to general law for religious practice and the problems this creates
Blasphemy laws — the philosophical argument against them and the political economy of why states keep them
Secularisation thesis — what it predicted, why it was wrong, what the revision says
recurring_motions_at_wudc_level: THW ban religious symbols in all public institutions
THBT religion does more harm than good
THW repeal all blasphemy laws
THBT the state should not fund religious schools
THBT religious belief should be irrelevant in political life

priority_topics.why_this_matters_for_debate: These motions are increasingly common and are often lost because debaters either argue from personal morality or from pure abstraction without connecting to social mechanisms. The key is understanding the state's interest in regulating intimate life and when that interest is legitimate.
the_mechanisms_to_understand: Why states regulate marriage — the economic, demographic, and social rationales
How loneliness is a structural phenomenon driven by urban design, work patterns, and digital displacement — not individual failure
How domestic violence operates as a system of power and control — not as occasional incidents
How hookup culture and the loneliness epidemic coexist — the paradox and its causes
How family structure interacts with economic inequality
key_concepts_own_these_precisely: Attachment theory — Bowlby's model and its implications for adult relationships
Social capital — Putnam's distinction between bridging and bonding capital
Power and control wheel — Duluth model of domestic violence
Companionate vs arranged marriage — the anthropological and historical view, not the stereotyped version
Loneliness epidemic — the data, the health implications, the structural causes
recurring_motions_at_wudc_level: THBT the state should not regulate marriage
THW legalise polyamorous civil unions
THBT social media is making intimate relationships worse
THW hold parents legally responsible for the crimes of their minor children

cross_domain_intersections.description: WUDC-level arguments live at intersections. A debater who can bring economic mechanisms into a feminism round, or bring philosophy into a criminal justice round, instantly operates at a higher level. The system should actively look for content that sits at these intersections.

high_value_intersections.what_to_look_for: surveillance infrastructure, algorithmic governance, digital authoritarianism, AI in policing
high_value_intersections.the_question_underneath: does technology strengthen or undermine democratic accountability

high_value_intersections.what_to_look_for: wealth concentration, inheritance, intergenerational mobility data, tax policy and redistribution
high_value_intersections.the_question_underneath: can capitalism produce justice or is equality of opportunity a fiction within capitalism

high_value_intersections.what_to_look_for: sanctions effectiveness, trade as foreign policy, IMF conditionality, debt and sovereignty
high_value_intersections.the_question_underneath: when does economic interdependence produce peace and when does it produce leverage

high_value_intersections.what_to_look_for: free will and responsibility, retribution vs rehabilitation, the philosophy of punishment
high_value_intersections.the_question_underneath: can punishment be justified if determinism is true

high_value_intersections.what_to_look_for: religious exemptions from anti-discrimination law, dress code debates, marriage law and religion
high_value_intersections.the_question_underneath: when religious freedom and gender equality conflict, which principle should prevail and by what method do you decide

high_value_intersections.what_to_look_for: automation and labour displacement, platform monopoly, data ownership and wealth
high_value_intersections.the_question_underneath: does the digital economy require new economic categories or just more aggressive application of existing ones

high_value_intersections.what_to_look_for: chip wars, AI arms race, cyber sovereignty, critical infrastructure vulnerabilities
high_value_intersections.the_question_underneath: is technological competition the new Cold War and does that frame help or distort

high_value_intersections.what_to_look_for: school funding inequality, legacy admissions, private school advantages, curriculum as ideology
high_value_intersections.the_question_underneath: is education a tool for social mobility or a mechanism for reproducing class

argument_architecture.description: For every article, every topic, every claim — build the argument in this structure. This is the template the coaching node should use.

wudc_argument_structure.claim: What you are asserting. One sentence. Precise.
wudc_argument_structure.warrant: Why it is true. The mechanism, the evidence, the logical chain. Not just an assertion restated.
wudc_argument_structure.impact: Why it matters. Probability x magnitude x reversibility. Who is affected and how much.
wudc_argument_structure.link_to_motion: How this argument wins the debate, not just makes a point.

clash_identification.description: Every debate has one or two central clashes. Find them before you build your case.
how_to_find_the_clash: Ask: what must both sides agree on for this to be a genuine disagreement
Ask: what value is on each side of this debate and are they really incompatible or just prioritised differently
Ask: what is the empirical question that, if settled, would settle the debate
Ask: what is the definitional question that, if settled, would change who wins

rebuttal_architecture.description: The three types of rebuttal, in order of power
types: Turn — their argument is not only wrong but actually supports your side
Take-out — their argument is wrong and here is why
Mitigate — their argument has some merit but it is outweighed by ours
rebuttal_architecture.the_golden_rule: Always steelman before you rebut. State their argument in its strongest form. Then rebut it. This is what WUDC judges reward.

english_language_development.description: This system is also a language development programme. Every output should model the English of a careful, precise, well-read thinker.

logical_connectives: the stronger version of this objection is
this holds true in most but not all cases
the distinction that matters here is
the premise is sound but the inference fails
the relevant comparison is not X but Y
hedging_expressions_that_signal_intellec: the evidence here is mixed, but the weight of it suggests
this is contested, and the honest answer is
I am granting the strongest version of this argument when I say
the distinction I am drawing is a fine one, but it matters because

sentence_patterns_to_internalise: X is true not because of A but because of B — and that distinction matters because it implies C.
The strongest version of the opposing argument is [steelman]. Here is why it still fails: [rebuttal].
The question is not whether X is true — it clearly is — but whether X is a reason to do Y.
Grant the premise. Deny the inference. The argument from X to Y requires assuming Z, which is exactly what is in dispute.
This is not a debate about whether harm exists. Both sides agree harm exists. The debate is about which harm is greater and which mechanism reliably reduces it.

weekly_knowledge_filter.description: The Weekend Agent's filtering rules. Be brutal. Most of what was read this week is noise. Identify the signal.
what_to_extract_and_keep_forever: Named concepts with an author or origin — these are the vocabulary of serious intellectual discourse
Theoretical frameworks with names — realism, compatibilism, subsidiarity, regulatory capture
Historical events that keep being cited as precedent in debates — the Melian Dialogue, the Marshall Plan, the Nuremberg principles
Statistics that are methodologically robust and durable — not one study, but the consensus of the literature
Value hierarchies — when liberty clashes with equality in a specific context, how do serious thinkers resolve it
Argument patterns with names — the slippery slope, the tu quoque, the false dilemma, the modus tollens
Definitional distinctions that change debate outcomes — rule of law vs rule by law, sex vs gender, equality vs equity
Philosophical positions and what they imply for specific policy questions
Mechanisms — the causal chains that explain why things happen, not just what happened
what_to_discard_immediately: Specific vote counts, seat numbers, or election results without systemic lessons
Economic data without context for what it proves
News events that happened to one person or institution with no broader lesson
Any piece of information that will be superseded by next week's news

debate_skill_targets.description: Content knowledge is necessary but not sufficient. These are the specific debate skills the system should develop through how it frames, argues, and coaches.

skills_with_how_to_develop_them.why_it_wins_debates: Motions are won and lost on definitions. If you define the key term, you set the terrain.
skills_with_how_to_develop_them.how_this_system_develops_it: For every topic, identify the 2-3 words whose definition determines who wins. The system extracts these.

skills_with_how_to_develop_them.why_it_wins_debates: The side that sets the burden correctly forces the opponent to play on unfavourable ground.
skills_with_how_to_develop_them.how_this_system_develops_it: Every coaching output identifies what must be proven and by whom.

skills_with_how_to_develop_them.why_it_wins_debates: Judges must choose between competing harms. The side that argues probability, magnitude, and reversibility most rigorously wins.
skills_with_how_to_develop_them.how_this_system_develops_it: Every argument concludes with an impact statement structured around these three dimensions.

skills_with_how_to_develop_them.why_it_wins_debates: Judges reward intellectual honesty. Acknowledging the best opposing argument before defeating it signals that you have thought carefully.
skills_with_how_to_develop_them.how_this_system_develops_it: Every coaching output includes the steelman version of the opposing case.

skills_with_how_to_develop_them.why_it_wins_debates: A concrete example from another country or historical period makes an abstract argument credible and memorable.
skills_with_how_to_develop_them.how_this_system_develops_it: Every topic digest includes at least two comparative examples.

skills_with_how_to_develop_them.why_it_wins_debates: Refusing to grant any ground looks defensive. Granting a point and showing it does not defeat your case looks secure.
skills_with_how_to_develop_them.how_this_system_develops_it: The rebuttal section always models the concede-and-rebuild structure.

skills_with_how_to_develop_them.why_it_wins_debates: Vague language signals vague thinking. Every word in a WUDC round should be the right word.
skills_with_how_to_develop_them.how_this_system_develops_it: Power phrases are written to demonstrate precision. The student absorbs the standard by reading it daily.
